# Code Changes

The workflow for going from an issue, pull request, idea, or feature request to shipped code.
Follow the phases in order. Analysis always comes first; code comes last.

Each phase has a reference file with the full instructions. **Read the reference file when you
enter the phase** — not before, and never skip it because the phase "looks obvious".

## Roles

Assign work based on model capability:

| Role         | Capability tier                               | Owns                                      |
| ------------ | --------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
| Orchestrator | Strongest reasoning model available           | Analysis, plan, supervision, verification |
| Implementer  | Mid-tier coder; small model for trivial edits | Executing one pinned, self-contained task |

Concrete model names per vendor (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google) and how to run the split in Claude
Code, GitHub Copilot, Codex, or IDE agents: [references/model-tiers.md](references/model-tiers.md).

When the current agent already runs on an implementer-tier model, skip delegation: plan and
execute directly, still following every phase. Never delegate to a model more capable than the
current one.

## The flow

1. **Analyze** ([references/analyze.md](references/analyze.md)) — root cause and scope,
   validated against the code, never against the report alone.
2. **Plan** ([references/plan.md](references/plan.md)) — pinned spec, task split, parallel vs
   sequential, workspace per task.
3. **Delegate** ([references/delegate.md](references/delegate.md)) — match each task to the
   cheapest capable executor.
4. **Supervise** ([references/supervise.md](references/supervise.md)) — monitor, unblock, and
   critically review implementer output.
5. **Verify** ([references/verify.md](references/verify.md)) — quality gates plus functional
   proof, never delegated.
6. **Deliver** ([references/deliver.md](references/deliver.md)) — conventional commits and an
   outcome-first report.

**Stop gate:** Phase 1 ends with reporting the analysis and proposed approach to the user and
waiting for a go. Skip the gate only when the user already gave the go in the request itself
("do it", "fix it and commit", "implement with Sonnet").

## Special cases

These entry points replace or extend Phase 1; the rest of the flow applies unchanged.

- Pull request review comments →
  [references/pr-review-comments.md](references/pr-review-comments.md)
- Issue triage → [references/issue-triage.md](references/issue-triage.md)

@MattKotsenas I'm reluctant to add something a single terminal "invented" without it being a convention, or am I wrong at that?
--
author:	MattKotsenas
association:	none
edited:	false
status:	none
--
Understood, though I'd argue most OSC codes are that way ;). 

`cmdline_url=` specifically has more traction than just Kitty; Ghostty has a full parser and test suite for both `cmdline=` and `cmdline_url=` (however, they don't emit it from their own shell yet that I can see). Other shells silently ignore unknown params on `133;C`, so backward compatibility is generally good.

I believe the reason adoption is limited is that *nix environments can use `tcgetpgrp` + `/proc/<pid>/cmdline`, so the pressure to solve this in-band is low. Windows has no equivalent; OSC codes are the only viable channel (that I know of).

Practically, enabling `shell_integration` in OMP today makes it _harder_ for users to polyfill this themselves. OMP's Enter key handler owns the `133;C` emission, and PSReadLine only supports one handler per key. A user wrapping `PSConsoleHostReadLine` independently would double-emit `133;C`.

As someone that wants this code for my workflow, I basically need to disable OMP shell integration and handle the codes myself. If I could only polyfill that single sequence myself I'd agree, but I thought it was at least worth asking, given that it's effectively "only" adding another key-value pair to a code OMP is _already_ emitting.

Let me know if this makes sense, or if you have any questions!
--
author:	JanDeDobbeleer
association:	owner
edited:	false
status:	none
--
It definitely makes sense. And I agree 100% that this entire feature is standard by accident rather than officially specced. I'll have a look! But we'd have to implement it wherever possible then.
--

[Feature Request] Include cmdline_url= parameter on OSC 133;C emission
OPEN
### What would you like to see added?

A small additive follow-up to #7441 (the PR that recently fixed `OSC 133;C` emission for PowerShell users with `shell_integration: true`):

**Extend the bare `OSC 133;C` emission to optionally carry the command line as a `cmdline_url=` parameter, matching kitty's published shell-integration convention.**

#### Background

[Kitty's shell integration scripts](https://sw.kovidgoyal.net/kitty/shell-integration/) extend the FinalTerm `OSC 133;C` state marker with a key=value parameter carrying the literal command line that just submitted. From their bash script:

```bash
builtin printf "\e]133;C;cmdline=%q\a" "$last_cmd"
```

And their fish script (because fish quoting differs):

```fish
printf '\e]133;C;cmdline_url=%s\a' (string escape --style=url -- "$argv")
```

The `cmdline=` form uses `%q` (POSIX shell quoting); `cmdline_url=` uses URL-percent-encoding. The latter is easier to round-trip from PowerShell (`[Uri]::EscapeDataString` produces it natively).

This convention is **backward-compatible by design**: terminals that don't parse the parameter see bare `OSC 133;C` (the same thing OMP emits today), so no consumer is broken. Terminals that do parse it get the literal command name without having to walk a process tree or guess.

#### Why this matters

`OSC 133;C` today tells the terminal "a command has started running" but doesn't say what command. Consumers that need the command name fall back to OS-level process inspection (`tcgetpgrp` + `/proc/<pid>/cmdline` on Unix), which:
- Doesn't exist on Windows (`ConPTY` has no foreground-process-group primitive; MSDN explicitly recommends against `GetConsoleProcessList` for this purpose)
- Is fragile even on Unix once tools nest wrappers (workers, language servers, subprocess trees)

Kitty's `cmdline_url=` extension closes the gap with one byte-string in the existing OSC 133;C emission.

Concrete downstream consumers include any tool that today does OS-level process inspection to answer "what command is running in this terminal". That includes terminal multiplexers (tmux's `pane_current_command`, Zellij, screen, and on Windows, psmux/psmux#299 which is currently designing its parser around exactly this cascade), terminal tab/title auto-rename (Windows Terminal, WezTerm, Ghostty, Konsole, gnome-terminal; all of which currently do their own process-tree inspection), command-block UIs (Warp, Tabby), and the existing scroll-to-prompt / view-output-in-pager features that kitty already ships against its own emission. OMP emitting this opens that whole consumer surface to every pwsh user with `shell_integration: true`, without requiring any of those tools to coordinate with OMP specifically.

#### Implementation shape

Update `New-EnterKeyHandler` in `src/shell/scripts/omp.ps1` to capture the command from PSReadLine's buffer state BEFORE calling AcceptLine, then include it as the `cmdline_url=` parameter:

```diff
function New-EnterKeyHandler {
    [scriptblock]$AcceptLineFunction,
+
+    $cmd = $null; $cursor = $null
+    if ($global:_ompFTCSMarks) {
+        [Microsoft.PowerShell.PSConsoleReadLine]::GetBufferState([ref]$cmd, [ref]$cursor)
+    }
+
    & $AcceptLineFunction
+
    if ($global:_ompFTCSMarks -and $executingCommand) {
+        if ($cmd) {
+            $encoded = [Uri]::EscapeDataString($cmd)
+            Write-Host "$([char]27)]133;C;cmdline_url=$encoded$([char]7)" -NoNewline
+        } else {
            Write-Host "$([char]27)]133;C$([char]7)" -NoNewline
+        }
+    }
}
```

Properties:
- Gated by the existing `_ompFTCSMarks` flag (driven by `shell_integration: true`)
- Backward compatible - terminals that don't parse the param see bare `OSC 133;C`, same as today
- Aligned with kitty's spec

#### What I'd contribute

Happy to send the PR if this lands as approved direction:
- The change above
- A unit test exercising the `cmdline_url=` emission path
- A note in the shell-integration docs

Let me know if you'd prefer `cmdline=` (POSIX-quoted) instead of `cmdline_url=`. Either fits the kitty convention; `cmdline_url=` was my pick because URL-percent-encoding round-trips cleanly through `[Uri]::EscapeDataString` / `[Uri]::UnescapeDataString` without needing a PowerShell-side equivalent of `%q`.

1	# Terminal
2	
3	## Windows Terminal and ConPTY
4	
5	- The "[process exited with code 0] / Ctrl+D to close" text flashing at shell exit is **Windows
6	  Terminal's own teardown message** (`ConptyConnection::_indicateExitWithStatus`) - visibility is
7	  a render/close race, never omp output.
8	- Panes launched via `wt.exe <commandline>` never auto-close under `closeOnExit=automatic`;
9	  profile-launched tabs do.
10	- On Windows 11 build 26300 a headless conhost reports cursor 0,0 / blank buffer and drops pty
11	  pipe input - visual row placement cannot be asserted headlessly, only state-machine behavior.
12	- pywinpty's ConPTY teardown EOF lags process death by a constant ~5s (harness artifact - it shows
13	  up in a plain-pwsh control too).
14	- SendKeys into Windows Terminal is unreliable while the user works - keystrokes get silently
15	  lost, so a "hung" test may simply never have received its `exit`.
16	
17	## Encoding
18	
19	- Under a stock CP437/ACP1252 console, pwsh's `&` call operator mangles UTF-8 program output
20	  (U+E0B6 becomes U+03B5). Reading a native program's UTF-8 output reliably requires
21	  `[Console]::OutputEncoding = UTF8` or the Process API.
22	- PowerShell `| Set-Content -NoNewline` joins piped lines into ONE line and corrupts scripts -
23	  write files with Git Bash redirection when byte fidelity matters.
24	
25	## Process facts
26	
27	- Windows process creation floor is ~70ms; a timer tick is 15.6ms (quantizes every short sleep).
28	- On Windows there is no SIGPIPE; child lifecycle management must rely on fd closure / stdin EOF.
29	- On native Linux, process spawns cost 11-16ms - daemon architectures that pay off on Windows can
30	  be a wash there (see the bash serve revert in [bash](bash.md)).

1	# PowerShell (pwsh)
2	
3	## Engine events and runspaces
4	
5	- `Register-EngineEvent` (e.g. `PowerShell.OnIdle`) `-Action` handlers, verified pwsh 7 (2026-07):
6	  - `-MessageData` is accepted but arrives as `$null` in the action (unlike `Register-ObjectEvent`,
7	    where it works).
8	  - `.GetNewClosure()` bindings are lost when the scriptblock is created inside a module function -
9	    captured variables resolve to `$null` when the action fires.
10	  - The only reliable way to pass state into an engine-event action is a `$global:` variable
11	    (see `$global:_ompStreamingState` in `src/shell/scripts/omp.ps1`).
12	- PSReadLine generates `PowerShell.OnIdle` itself every ~300-450ms while waiting for input (only
13	  when the input buffer is empty) and pumps ALL queued subscriber actions via a nested pipeline.
14	  `InvokePrompt()` is designed for OnIdle subscribers.
15	- PSEvents raised from a NON-engine thread (e.g. `Register-ObjectEvent DataAdded` on a collection
16	  appended by a background runspace) can re-enter `RunspaceBase.Pulse()` and **crash the host**
17	  with InvalidPipelineStateException under rapid prompt cycles. Consume records only on the engine
18	  thread: sync waiter plus OnIdle drain.
19	- Whether `Register-ObjectEvent` actions fire during a busy-wait depends on where the wait runs
20	  (top-level script vs busy pipeline thread) - it is not a given either way. Never share
21	  a consume-cursor between an event action and a synchronous waiter - give the waiter a private
22	  cursor (records stay in the PSDataCollection) and make the action idempotent.
23	- OnIdle needs ~300ms of idle time. Anything the user can trigger sooner (transient prompt on a
24	  fast Enter) must ALSO drain synchronously at its call site or it pays a CLI-spawn fallback.
25	
26	## Exit lifecycle
27	
28	- pwsh cannot exit while a `[powershell]::Create()` pipeline thread runs - pipeline threads are
29	  foreground threads. A reader runspace blocked in `ReadByte()` on a child's stdout deadlocks
30	  `exit` when that child exits only on stdin EOF (issue #7643).
31	- Module `OnRemove` only runs on `Remove-Module`, never on normal `exit` - it cannot be a
32	  daemon's teardown path. Use a `Register-EngineEvent PowerShell.Exiting` handler that writes
33	  `quit`, **closes the child's stdin** (the guaranteed EOF signal), and kills after a short
34	  `WaitForExit`. State reaches the action via `$global:` (see above).
35	
36	## Performance (measured 2026-07-06, ARM64 Windows 11)
37	
38	- Process creation floor is ~70ms (`cmd /c exit`); any omp spawn from pwsh costs ~100-130ms wall
39	  regardless of exe speed. When touching `omp.ps1` perf, count process spawns per Enter first -
40	  the spawn-per-prompt architecture dominates, script/module cmdlet overhead is negligible
41	  (<0.1ms; module vs plain script is a non-issue).
42	- `&` call operator vs the Process API is only ~10-17ms faster - CreateProcess dominates. Under a
43	  stock CP437/1252 console, `&` mangles UTF-8 output (U+E0B6 becomes U+03B5); dropping the Process
44	  API requires `[Console]::OutputEncoding = UTF8` once at init.
45	- Serve daemon warm render ~10ms; warm prompt cost 18-23ms vs 160-220ms for the per-prompt
46	  streaming cycle it replaced (runspace creation ~35ms + event churn + 15.6ms-quantized sleeps).
47	- Windows timer resolution: each `Start-Sleep -Milliseconds 1` is a ~15.6ms tick - sleep-polling
48	  loops are ~16x slower than intended. Prefer a `ManualResetEventSlim` signaled by the reader.
49	
50	## Testing
51	
52	- `New-Event -SourceIdentifier PowerShell.OnIdle` runs the real OnIdle action deterministically
53	  (engines never idle mid-script). Beware the action's side effects short-circuiting later prompt
54	  calls in the harness.
55	- Set module-scope flags from a test: `& (Get-Module oh-my-posh-core) { $script:X = $true }`.
56	- pwsh 5.1 / ConstrainedLanguage keep the legacy stream path - serve is gated to pwsh 6+.
57	- Exit-deadlock repro pattern: `Start-Process pwsh -File test.ps1` + `WaitForExit(timeout)`;
58	  `findstr x` with redirected stdio is a good stand-in daemon.
59

1	# zsh
2	
3	## zle facts
4	
5	- Every new editor invocation starts in keymap `main`; a `vicmd` selection does not survive into
6	  the next line.
7	- `$?` after the transient prompt's `zle .send-break` is 1; native Ctrl+C yields 130. Known gap,
8	  documented as a TODO in `_omp_zle-line-init` - do not "fix" one without solving the other path.
9	- Ubuntu's `/etc/zsh/zshrc` defines a `zle-line-init` (terminfo smkx), so omp's widget takes the
10	  decorate path even in minimal setups - never assume the widget slot is empty.
11	
12	## zsh-vi-mode (ZVM) interaction (verified 2026-07-14, issue #5992)
13	
14	- ZVM initializes lazily at first `precmd`, so it ALWAYS wraps omp's `zle-line-init` regardless of
15	  source order: its wrapper runs our widget first, `zvm_zle-line-init` second. Because the
16	  transient prompt's `zle .recursive-edit` consumes the whole editing session, ZVM's line-init
17	  effectively ran at line END - any line accepted or interrupted from normal mode desynced
18	  `ZVM_MODE` from the active keymap, and `zvm_select_vi_mode`'s same-mode early return made the
19	  break permanent.
20	- Fix in `_omp_zle-line-init`: call `zvm_zle-line-init` up front (guarded on
21	  `$+functions[zvm_zle-line-init]` and `ZVM_INIT_DONE == true`). Keep this if the function is ever
22	  restructured.
23	- Landmine: `zvm_reset_prompt` resolves `$rawfunc` via **dynamic scoping**. Any ZVM code running
24	  `zle reset-prompt` while inside our line-init picks up the line-init wrapper's `rawfunc`
25	  (`_omp_decorated_zle-line-init`) and re-enters the widget recursively. The `local rawfunc=` at
26	  the top of `_omp_zle-line-init` shadows it - load-bearing, keep it.
27	
28	## coproc and signals
29	
30	- An interactive zsh with MONITOR prints "[n] pid" at coproc spawn; `disown` is too late and a
31	  `{ coproc ... } 2>/dev/null` block does NOT suppress it. `setopt localoptions no_monitor` does -
32	  side effect: the child inherits SIGINT/SIGQUIT ignored (POSIX no-job-control), which Go
33	  preserves.
34	- Duplicate coproc fds to session fds (`exec {out}<&p {in}>&p`) - duplicates survive a later
35	  `coproc` replacing the slot. `disown %+` keeps the daemon out of `jobs` and the job-count
36	  segment.
37	- Writing to a dead coproc pipe raises SIGPIPE, which **kills a non-interactive zsh outright**
38	  (`2>/dev/null` cannot stop a signal). Guard daemon writes with a `kill -0 $pid` pre-check plus
39	  `setopt localoptions localtraps; trap '' PIPE` (function-local, user pipelines unaffected).
40	- Never pass a possibly-zero pid to `kill -0` - `kill -0 0` signals the caller's own process group
41	  and always succeeds.
42	
43	## Footguns
44	
45	- A redirection-only `exec` applies EVERY listed redirection to the shell permanently:
46	  `exec {fd}<&p {fd}>&p 2>/dev/null` silences the session's stderr for good (caused issue #7653).
47	  Scope the stderr suppression with a brace block: `{ exec ... } 2>/dev/null`.
48	- zsh 5.9: `read -r -u $fd -d $'\0' -t N` ignores `-t` entirely and blocks forever on a silent fd.
49	  Without `-d`, the timeout works.
50	- Teardown belongs in `zshexit_functions`; the daemon lifecycle is fd-governed (closing the fds -
51	  or the shell dying, even by SIGKILL - EOFs the daemon's stdin).
52	- To debug widget re-entry, log `${funcstack[*]}` inside the widget - `zle` calls from shell
53	  functions appear on the stack and expose who invoked what.
54

1	# bash
2	
3	Verified on bash 5.2 (2026-07).
4	
5	## Readline and prompt
6	
7	- Running the `exec` builtin from PROMPT_COMMAND - even a harmless `exec {fd}</dev/null` -
8	  **silently disables readline for the whole session**: the prompt never paints again, no error,
9	  commands still execute non-interactively. Use coproc fds directly; never exec-dup or exec-close
10	  from prompt context.
11	- PS1 must stay `'$(_omp_get_primary)'` (single-quoted). With promptvars on, literal prompt
12	  content executes `$(...)` - a directory named `$(cmd)` becomes command injection.
13	- "Expansion returns correct bytes" is NOT "prompt displays" - always capture a real typescript
14	  (`script -qe -c ... FILE`) when verifying prompt behavior. Note `script(1)`'s stdout relay does
15	  not carry bash prompt bytes (zsh's does); probe `${PS1@P}` in-session instead.
16	
17	## coproc
18	
19	- `coproc NAME { cmd; }` reports the pid of a wrapper subshell - `exec` in the body so the daemon
20	  replaces it.
21	- The coproc's ORIGINAL fds must be closed after duplication
22	  (`eval "exec ${NAME[0]}<&- ${NAME[1]}>&-"`) or the child's stdin never EOFs when the dups close.
23	- Subshells close coproc fds in non-interactive bash - a `(trap '' PIPE; ...)` subshell guard does
24	  not work there; save/ignore/restore `trap '' PIPE` in the parent instead (nothing forks while
25	  ignored).
26	- bash prints failed `>&fd` redirection errors before later redirections apply - put
27	  `2>/dev/null` BEFORE `>&"$fd"`.
28	
29	## History
30	
31	- A bash serve daemon was implemented and **reverted** (2026-07-07): no measurable speedup -
32	  native-Linux spawns are 11-16ms and sync-only wait-mode plus the display-time subshell cannot
33	  beat that. Do not re-propose without new evidence. The gotchas above came out of that work and
34	  remain valid.
35

1	# fish
2	
3	Verified on fish 4.1.2 in WSL (2026-07).
4	
5	## Process and job model
6	
7	- fish does NOT fork a backgrounded pipeline stage that is a *function* - it blocks the main
8	  shell. Run readers as an external process: `fish --no-config -c $script args &`.
9	- fish 4.x `jobs --last --pid` prints a "Process" header plus one pid per pipeline stage - filter
10	  with `string match --regex '^\d+$'` and treat the result as a list.
11	- `kill -0 0` always succeeds (signals the caller's own process group) - guard zero pids
12	  everywhere before liveness checks.
13	
14	## Fifos and lifecycle
15	
16	- A fifo write with no reader blocks forever - liveness-check the reader before every fifo write.
17	- The serve daemon opens its request fifo O_RDWR so fish's open-write-close pattern never EOFs it;
18	  consequence: SIGKILL of fish orphans daemon and reader permanently (no EOF, no SIGPIPE ever).
19	  Normal teardown runs via the `fish_exit` event handler (quit + kill the pipeline).
20	- `--on-signal SIGUSR1` handlers fire between commands in non-interactive scripts (verified with a
21	  200ms poll loop).
22	
23	## Testing quirks
24	
25	- fish under `script(1)` discards/paste-buffers piped typeahead, so in-session probes never
26	  execute - a harness limitation, not a bug (zsh under the same setup is fine). Assert via files
27	  written by event handlers instead.
28	- The streaming transient prompt is cached in a tempfile (`$_omp_streaming_tempfile.transient`),
29	  not a variable, because `_omp_cleanup_stream` runs before the transient repaint.
30

1	# cmd / Clink
2	
3	## Lua and pipes
4	
5	- Clink's `io.popenrw` reads are BLOCKING with no peek/timeout in Clink Lua (verified in
6	  `io_api.cpp`). Any protocol consumed from Lua must guarantee a fixed number of records per
7	  request - the serve wait-mode contract (exactly 2 records, upheld by Go's `renderComplete` even
8	  on segment panic) exists for this.
9	- `io.popenrw` runs the command via `%COMSPEC% /c`, so `2>nul` works in the command string and is
10	  REQUIRED - the child's stderr otherwise inherits the console and corrupts the display.
11	- Clink creates its pipes `_O_NOINHERIT` and only dups the child ends inheritable
12	  (`pipe_pair::init`), so cmd's death guarantees the daemon's stdin write handle closes.
13	
14	## Windows lifecycle
15	
16	- There is no SIGPIPE on Windows - stdin EOF is a daemon's ONLY exit signal. Design teardown
17	  around fd closure, not signals.
18	- The cmd feature line for the daemon is `serve_enabled = true` (Streaming feature in
19	  `src/shell/cmd.go`).
20	
21	## Testing
22	
23	- `luac -p` for syntax; a Lua harness with a stubbed Clink API covers logic
24	  (lua.exe via `winget install DEVCOM.Lua`, Clink via `winget install chrisant996.Clink`).
25	- Clink itself cannot run headless - live smoke tests stay manual.

1	# Phase 2 — Plan
2	
3	Turn the approved analysis into an executable plan. The quality bar: an implementer-tier model
4	must be able to execute each task without asking questions.
5	
6	## Pin the spec
7	
8	Write the spec down before delegating anything. It contains:
9	
10	- The decided approach — including decisions already made, so the implementer does not relitigate
11	  them.
12	- Files to touch, and the entry points to start from.
13	- Constraints: style rules, patterns to follow (point at existing code), performance or
14	  compatibility requirements.
15	- Verification commands the implementer must run locally (build, tests, lint).
16	- Explicit non-goals: what the task must NOT change. This is what keeps subagents from wandering.
17	
18	## Split into tasks
19	
20	- One task = one self-contained unit an implementer can finish and verify on its own.
21	- Mark which tasks are independent and which consume another task's output. Parallelize the
22	  independent ones; sequence the rest. When in doubt, sequence — a merge conflict between two
23	  parallel subagents costs more than the parallelism saves.
24	- Documentation updates belong to the task that changes the behavior, not to a separate task.
25	
26	## Decide the workspace per task
27	
28	- Main working tree: when the task depends on uncommitted local changes, or when you will review
29	  and commit the result in the current session.
30	- Isolated worktree: everything else, especially parallel tasks — they must never share a
31	  working tree.
32	
33	## Output of this phase
34	
35	A task list where each entry names its executor tier (see Phase 3), its workspace, its
36	dependencies, and carries its pinned spec.
37

1	# Phase 3 — Delegate
2	
3	Match the executor to the task, not the other way around. The point is cost and speed without
4	losing quality — the orchestrator stays accountable for the result.
5	
6	## Executor matrix
7	
8	Tier definitions and per-vendor model examples: [model-tiers.md](model-tiers.md).
9	
10	| Task profile                                             | Executor                                         |
11	| -------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ |
12	| Trivial: mechanical edit, config tweak, typo, doc update | Do it directly, or batch on a trivial-tier model |
13	| Standard, well-specified implementation                  | Implementer-tier subagent                        |
14	| Ambiguous, architectural, or judgment-heavy              | Orchestrator keeps it                            |
15	
16	- Launch independent tasks as parallel subagents, each in its own worktree.
17	- When the current agent is itself implementer-tier, there is no further delegation: execute
18	  directly, following the same spec discipline.
19	
20	## What a delegation carries
21	
22	Hand the subagent its full pinned spec from Phase 2, plus:
23	
24	- The verification commands it must run and pass before reporting done.
25	- The instruction to report what it changed and what it verified — not just "done".
26	- The instruction to stop and report when it hits something the spec does not cover, instead of
27	  improvising scope.
28	
29	## What is never outsourced
30	
31	The orchestrator never delegates:
32	
33	- Analysis and root-cause work (Phase 1).
34	- Final verification (Phase 5) — an implementer's green run is a claim, not a result.
35	- Commits, history rewrites, pushes, and user-facing reporting (Phase 6).

23	# A persistent `oh-my-posh serve` process renders prompts on request,
24	# replacing a process spawn per prompt with an in-memory render. Requests go
25	# through a named pipe (the only way fish can write to a running process);
26	# records flow back through a background reader into tempfiles.
27	set --global _omp_serve_pid 0
28	
29	# _omp_serve_pid holds every pid of the daemon pipeline (fish's
30	# `jobs --last --pid` lists one per stage). The first is the daemon itself
31	# and serves as the liveness proxy; kill/stop target all of them.
32	#
33	# NEVER pass a possibly-zero pid to kill: `kill -0 0` signals the caller's
34	# own process group and always succeeds.
35	function _omp_serve_alive
36	    test -n "$_omp_serve_pid[1]" -a "$_omp_serve_pid[1]" -gt 0 2>/dev/null
37	    and kill -0 $_omp_serve_pid[1] 2>/dev/null
38	end
39	set --global _omp_serve_fifo ''
40	set --global _omp_serve_tempfile ''

input.prompt:
You are implementing a pinned spec in the repo at D:\oh-my-posh (branch `next`, clean tree). Do NOT commit. Do NOT change anything beyond what this spec pins. If something in the spec doesn't match the actual file content, stop and report instead of improvising.

# Feature
Extend every `OSC 133;C` (FTCS_COMMAND_EXECUTED) emission in the shell init scripts to carry the just-submitted command line as `cmdline_url=<percent-encoded command>` (kitty shell-integration convention, RFC 3986 percent-encoding, unreserved set `A-Za-z0-9 . _ ~ -` kept literal, everything else `%XX` byte-wise). When the command is empty/unavailable, emit the bare `133;C` exactly as today. Encoding must happen in-shell with builtins only — never spawn a process for it.

Six script files + one docs file. Apply the exact code below, matching each file's existing indentation style.

## 1. src/shell/scripts/omp.ps1 — New-EnterKeyHandler (around line 1102)
Current scriptblock body (inside `New-EnterKeyHandler`):
```powershell
            return {
                try {
                    $Streaming.State = 'NEW'
                    $parseErrors = $null
                    [Microsoft.PowerShell.PSConsoleReadLine]::GetBufferState([ref]$null, [ref]$null, [ref]$parseErrors, [ref]$null)
                    $executingCommand = $parseErrors.Count -eq 0
                    if ($global:_ompTransientPrompt -and $executingCommand) {
                        Set-TransientPrompt
                    }
                }
                finally {
                    & $AcceptLineFunction
                    if ($global:_ompFTCSMarks -and $executingCommand) {
                        # Write FTCS_COMMAND_EXECUTED after accepting the input - it should still happen before execution
                        Write-Host "$([char]27)]133;C$([char]7)" -NoNewline
                    }
                }
            }.GetNewClosure()
```
Replace with:
```powershell
            return {
                try {
                    $Streaming.State = 'NEW'
                    $ast = $null
                    $parseErrors = $null
                    [Microsoft.PowerShell.PSConsoleReadLine]::GetBufferState([ref]$ast, [ref]$null, [ref]$parseErrors, [ref]$null)
                    $executingCommand = $parseErrors.Count -eq 0
                    if ($global:_ompTransientPrompt -and $executingCommand) {
                        Set-TransientPrompt
                    }
                }
                finally {
                    & $AcceptLineFunction
                    if ($global:_ompFTCSMarks -and $executingCommand) {
                        # Write FTCS_COMMAND_EXECUTED after accepting the input - it should still happen before execution.
                        # The command line rides along as kitty's cmdline_url= extension, percent-encoded.
                        # Windows PowerShell's Uri.EscapeDataString throws beyond 32766 characters, hence the length cap.
                        $cmdline = ''
                        $command = $ast.Extent.Text
                        if ($command -and $command.Length -lt 32000) {
                            $cmdline = ";cmdline_url=$([Uri]::EscapeDataString($command))"
                        }
                        Write-Host "$([char]27)]133;C$cmdline$([char]7)" -NoNewline
                    }
                }
            }.GetNewClosure()
```

```zsh
# percent-encode $1 into REPLY, byte-wise, keeping RFC 3986 unreserved characters literal
function _omp_urlencode() {
  emulate -L zsh
  setopt no_multibyte
  local str=$1 ch
  local -i i
  REPLY=''
  for (( i = 1; i <= ${#str}; i++ )); do
    ch=$str[i]
    if [[ $ch == [A-Za-z0-9._~-] ]]; then
      REPLY+=$ch
      continue
    fi
    printf -v ch '%%%02X' "'$ch"

  done
}

function _omp_preexec() {
  if [[ $_omp_ftcs_marks == 1 ]]; then
    if [[ -n $1 ]]; then
```

## 3. src/shell/scripts/omp.bash — _omp_ftcs_command_start (around line 68)

Replace with:
```bash
# percent-encode $1, byte-wise, keeping RFC 3986 unreserved characters literal
function _omp_urlencode() {
    local LC_ALL=C
    local str=$1 encoded='' ch i
    for ((i = 0; i < ${#str}; i++)); do
        ch=${str:i:1}
        case $ch in
        [A-Za-z0-9._~-])
            encoded+=$ch
            ;;
        *)
            printf -v ch '%%%02X' "'$ch"
            encoded+=$ch
            ;;
        esac
    done
    printf '%s' "$encoded"
}

function _omp_ftcs_command_start() {
    if [[ $_omp_ftcs_marks != 1 ]]; then
        return
    fi

    # the command comes from history: format is "  501  command", or
    # "  501* command" when the entry was modified; commands are missing
    # entirely when history is off or HISTCONTROL ignores them
    local cmd=''
    if [[ -o history ]]; then

        cmd=${cmd#"${cmd%%[!0-9]*}"}       # strip the history number
        cmd=${cmd#??}                      # strip the modified flag and separator
    fi

    if [[ -n $cmd ]]; then
        # advertise the command line via kitty's cmdline_url= extension
        printf '\e]133;C;cmdline_url=%s\a' "$(_omp_urlencode "$cmd")"
        return
    fi

    printf '\e]133;C\a'
}
```

```lua
    if string.gsub(input, '^%s*(.-)%s*$', '%1') ~= '' then
```

Base directory for this skill: D:\oh-my-posh\.claude\skills\conventional-commit

# Conventional Commit

## Commit Message Structure

```text
<type>(<scope>): <description>

[optional body]

[optional footer(s)]
```

### Types

| Type       | When to use                                            |
| ---------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| `feat`     | A new feature                                          |
| `fix`      | A bug fix                                              |
| `docs`     | Documentation changes, no code                         |
| `style`    | Formatting, missing semicolons, etc. (no logic change) |
| `refactor` | Code change that is neither a fix nor a feature        |
| `perf`     | Performance improvement                                |
| `test`     | Adding or correcting tests                             |
| `ci`       | CI configuration changes                               |
| `chore`    | Maintenance tasks (updating deps, tooling, etc.)       |
| `revert`   | Reverts a previous commit                              |

Append `!` after the type/scope to signal a **breaking change**: `feat!:` or `feat(api)!:`
When a change breaks existing behavior, **both markers are mandatory**: the `!` suffix on the type
**and** the `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer. They always appear together — never one without the other.

### Scope

Optional. Use the name of the area affected, e.g., `segment`, `cache`, `config`, `ui`.
Omit when the change is truly cross-cutting.

### Description

- Required. One short imperative sentence, no period at the end. The full header line (type +
  scope + description) must be **72 characters or fewer**. Aim for **50 characters or fewer for
  the description itself** — this almost always keeps the full header within budget regardless
  of type and scope length.
- Use the imperative mood: "add", not "added" or "adds". Never past tense or present-third-person:
  ✗ `added`, `fixed`, `bumped`, `implemented` → ✓ `add`, `fix`, `bump`, `implement`.
- **Never mirror the input's phrasing.** If the request uses past-tense words (`updated`, `added`,
  `bumped`, `was removed`, `got regenerated`), convert them to imperative before writing the
  description: `update`, `add`, `bump`, `remove`, `regenerate`.

### Body

Optional. Add context about _why_ the change was made, not _what_. The diff shows that.
Wrap at 72 characters.

### Footer

Use for:

- `BREAKING CHANGE: <description>` (required when `!` is used; explains the break).
- Issue references: `Closes #123`, `Fixes #456`.
- Co-authors: `Co-Authored-By: Name <email>`.

## Workflow

1. Run `git status` to review changed files.
2. Run `git diff` and `git diff --cached` to inspect staged and unstaged changes.
3. Identify the **type** from the table above. Ask yourself: does this change **remove, rename, or
   alter existing behavior** that callers depend on? If yes → it is a breaking change: use `!`
   after the type/scope **and** add a `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer. Both markers are always required
   together.
4. Identify the **scope** from the files/area changed.
5. Write a short **description** in the imperative mood.
6. Add a **body** if the _why_ needs explanation.
7. Add a **footer** for breaking changes or issue references.
8. Stage the relevant files explicitly (avoid `git add -A`).
9. Commit with a message that preserves multi-line formatting when body/footer are present.

## Examples

```text
feat(segment): add Ramadan segment with Aladhan API
fix(cache): always store mod time
docs(readme): update installation instructions
refactor(config): simplify option parsing logic
chore(deps): bump github.com/shirou/gopsutil/v4
feat(segment)!: rename template property StartTime to Start

BREAKING CHANGE: template strings using .StartTime must be updated to .Start
```

## Validation Checklist

- [ ] Type is one of the allowed values in .commitlintrc.yml
- [ ] The commit message respects the rules defined in .commitlintrc.yml
- [ ] Scope (if present) reflects the actual area changed
- [ ] Description is imperative mood, no trailing period
- [ ] Full header line (type + scope + description) is 72 characters or fewer
- [ ] Both `!` after type/scope **and** `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer are present whenever the change breaks existing behavior
- [ ] No sensitive files staged (.env, credentials, etc.)

Extend the OSC 133;C emission with the just-submitted command line,
percent-encoded as kitty's cmdline_url= shell-integration extension.
Terminals and multiplexers no longer need process-tree inspection to
know what command a pane runs - a primitive Windows lacks entirely.
Encoding happens in-shell with builtins only, so no extra process
spawn per prompt; terminals that ignore the parameter still see the
bare mark.

Closes #7536

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
